coredata.dc:title: Optimizing the microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of blue-emitting l-cysteine-derived carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yoshinaga, Taishu
Iso, Yoshiki
Isobe, Tetsuhiko
coredata.dc:description: We synthesized carbon dots (CDs) from biocompatible l-cysteine, containing N and S, through an eco-friendly, facile hydrothermal reaction. These l-cysteine-derived CDs are produced by pyrolysis and carbonization after dehydration between NH2 and COOH groups of l-cysteine. To optimize the microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of the blue-emitting l-cysteine-derived CDs, we varied the hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal duration, synthesis pH, and l-cysteine concentration. We synthesized CDs from 17 to 133 mmol L−1 aqueous solutions of l-cysteine whose pH values were 1, 7, or 13 through hydrothermal treatment at 110–230 °C for 5–120 min using a microwave heater. The absorbance of transitions related to CC, CO, and CN bonds increased through hydrothermal treatment at increasing temperature and for increasing duration. At the same time, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity and PL quantum yield (QY) of blue emission from the CDs under near-UV excitation increased. We propose that higher temperature and longer duration promote the carbonization, oxidation, and doping of nitrogen into CDs, resulting in the formation of π levels, surface state levels related to functional groups, and N-doped levels, respectively. The absorbance and PL intensity of the CDs prepared at pH 7 were the highest among the pH values 1, 7, and 13. The protonation of NH2 groups at pH 1 and the deprotonation of COOH groups at pH 13 suppressed dehydration between the NH2 and COOH groups of l-cysteine. In contrast, rapid dehydration at pH 7 induced carbonization, contributing to the maximized absorbance and PL intensity. The absorbance, PL intensity, and PL QY increased through hydrothermal treatment at increasing l-cysteine concentration. Higher l-cysteine concentration promoted dehydration between molecules and the subsequent carbonization, improving the optical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Carbon dots
l-cysteine
Hydrothermal treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of hydrothermal temperature on formation of CDs
Influence of hydrothermal duration on formation of CDs
Influence of pH of l-cysteine solution on formation of CDs
Influence of l-cysteine concentration on formation of CDs
Influence of heating systems on PL intensity of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) l-cysteine solution and CDs dispersions (measurement conditions: 100 mg L−1 and pH 7) prepared under different synthesis conditions. (A) T (°C): (b) 110, (c) 140, (d) 170, (e) 200, and (f) 230. (B) t (min): (b) 5, (c) 15, (d) 30, (e) 60, (f) 90, and (g) 120. (C) y: (b) 1, (c) 7, and (d) 13. (D) x (mmol L−1): (b) 17, (c) 33, (d) 67, (e) 100, and (f) 133.

PL spectra (λex: 360 nm) and photographs (λex: 365 nm) of CDs dispersions (measurement conditions: 100 mg L−1 and pH 7) prepared under different synthesis conditions. (A) T (°C): (a) 110, (b) 140, (c) 170, (d) 200, and (e) 230. (B) t (min): (a) 5, (b) 15, (c) 30, (d) 60, (e) 90, and (f) 120. (C) y: (a) 1, (b) 7, and (c) 13. (D) x (mmol L−1): (a) 17, (b) 33, (c) 67, (d) 100, and (e) 133.
Absolute PL quantum yields of CDs dispersions prepared under different conditions of (A) hydrothermal temperature, (B) hydrothermal duration, (C) synthesis pH, and (D) l-cysteine concentration.

Changes in N/C (red circle) and S/C (blue square) molar ratios as functions of (A) synthesis pH and (B) l-cysteine concentration. The N/C and S/C molar ratios, 0.33, of l-cysteine molecules are shown as a broken line. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A–E) TEM images of CDs. (F–J) Particle size distributions measured from the TEM images. l-cysteine concentration (mmol L−1): (A, F) 17, (B, G) 33, (C, H) 67, (D, I) 100, and (E, J) 133. (K) Changes in mean particle size and its standard deviation with l-cysteine concentration.

FT-IR spectra of (a) l-cysteine and (b–f) CDs powder samples prepared at different l-cysteine concentrations in the regions of (A) 400–4000 cm−1 and (B) 400–2000 cm−1. l-cysteine concentration (mmol L−1): (b) 17, (c) 33, (d) 67, (e) 100, and (f) 133.
Relationship between the formation process of CDs and the properties of CDs prepared at different l-cysteine concentrations.

head.ce:title: Optimizing the microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of blue-emitting l-cysteine-derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Keio University, 3-14-1 Hiyoshi, Kohoku-ku, Yokohama, 223-8522, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Keio University, 3-14-1 Hiyoshi, Kohoku-ku, Yokohama 223-8522, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We synthesized carbon dots (CDs) from biocompatible l-cysteine, containing N and S, through an eco-friendly, facile hydrothermal reaction. These l-cysteine-derived CDs are produced by pyrolysis and carbonization after dehydration between NH2 and COOH groups of l-cysteine. To optimize the microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of the blue-emitting l-cysteine-derived CDs, we varied the hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal duration, synthesis pH, and l-cysteine concentration. We synthesized CDs from 17 to 133 mmol L−1 aqueous solutions of l-cysteine whose pH values were 1, 7, or 13 through hydrothermal treatment at 110–230 °C for 5–120 min using a microwave heater. The absorbance of transitions related to CC, CO, and CN bonds increased through hydrothermal treatment at increasing temperature and for increasing duration. At the same time, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity and PL quantum yield (QY) of blue emission from the CDs under near-UV excitation increased. We propose that higher temperature and longer duration promote the carbonization, oxidation, and doping of nitrogen into CDs, resulting in the formation of π levels, surface state levels related to functional groups, and N-doped levels, respectively. The absorbance and PL intensity of the CDs prepared at pH 7 were the highest among the pH values 1, 7, and 13. The protonation of NH2 groups at pH 1 and the deprotonation of COOH groups at pH 13 suppressed dehydration between the NH2 and COOH groups of l-cysteine. In contrast, rapid dehydration at pH 7 induced carbonization, contributing to the maximized absorbance and PL intensity. The absorbance, PL intensity, and PL QY increased through hydrothermal treatment at increasing l-cysteine concentration. Higher l-cysteine concentration promoted dehydration between molecules and the subsequent carbonization, improving the optical properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have received attention as environmentally friendly nanomaterials because of their favorable characteristics of low toxicity, availability of a variety of carbon sources, good dispersion in water, and visible emission [1–7]. Benefiting from these advantages, applications of CDs have been demonstrated in a wide range of fields, including bioimaging [8], drug delivery [9], detection of heavy metals [10,11], thermal sensors [2,12], photocatalysts [13], and light-emitting diodes [14].

CDs can be produced from a variety of carbon sources through a hydrothermal reaction, which is an environmentally friendly, facile synthetic route [2,8,15–18]. We have focused on amino acids as carbon sources because they have well defined molecular structures (Fig. 1 ), in contrast to natural raw materials such as orange juice [19,20], grass [21], and plants [4].

To explore a promising carbon source for fluorescent CDs, we hydrothermally treated aqueous solutions of the amino acids shown in Fig. S1 at 230 °C for 30 min, using a microwave heater. The l-cysteine-derived CDs were found to exhibit the strongest blue emission under near-UV excitation among all the samples after hydrothermal synthesis (Figs. S2 and S3). l-cysteine contains not only nitrogen, which is found in all amino acids, but also sulfur in its molecular structure, and these elements serve as heteroatom dopants with appropriate energy levels to enhance the blue emission intensity [22,23]; therefore, we chose l-cysteine as a carbon source in this study.

Wei et al. hydrothermally treated a ∼275 mmol L−1 aqueous solution of l-cysteine at 180 °C for 12 h to yield CDs with a relative photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 12.6% [24]. Li et al. hydrothermally treated a higher concentration, 1000 mmol L−1, of l-cysteine aqueous solution at higher temperature, 300 °C, for 2 h using a stainless steel autoclave to yield CDs with a relative PL QY of 13.2% [25]. These l-cysteine-derived CDs were composed of C, H, N, S, and O, and had CC, CO, CN, C–N, and N–H bonds. In the absorption spectra of these CDs, π→π∗ transitions of CC and n→π∗ transitions of CO and CN were observed [24]. These l-cysteine-derived CDs exhibited blue emission under near UV light excitation. However, optimization of the hydrothermal synthesis of l-cysteine-derived CDs has never been investigated.

In the present study, to optimize the hydrothermal synthesis of blue-emitting l-cysteine-derived CDs, we investigated the influence of the synthesis parameters of hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal duration, synthesis pH, and l-cysteine concentration on the properties of the CDs. The above-mentioned hydrothermal syntheses of l-cysteine-derived CDs used electric heating systems, while a microwave heating system has not previously been used to our best knowledge. We used a microwave heating system as this allows the reaction solution to be more quickly and uniformly heated [26].
l-cysteine (98.0%), hydrochloric acid (35.0–37.0%), and sodium hydroxide (97.0%) were purchased from Wako Pure Chemical. The reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

A schematic illustration of the preparation method is shown in Supplementary Material, Fig. S4. An l-cysteine aqueous solution (x mmol L−1; 17 ≤ x ≤ 133) whose pH was adjusted to be y (y = 1, 7, or 13) was purged with Ar gas for 30 min at a flow rate of 300 mL min−1. Then, a sealed polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) vessel (Berghof, DAK-70) containing the solution was heated at T °C (110 ≤ T ≤ 230) for t min (5 ≤ t ≤ 120) using an autoclave with a microwave heating system (Berghof, Speedwave MWS-2) whose maximum operating temperature, i.e., upper limit temperature was 230 °C. After cooling the PTFE vessel to room temperature by placing it in water, the obtained suspension was centrifuged at ∼16,000×g (12,000 rpm using a rotor with a diameter of 10 cm) for 15 min to remove large particles of by-products. The obtained supernatant solution was passed through a membrane filter (pore size 0.2 μm) to yield an aqueous CDs dispersion. A powder sample was obtained by freeze-drying the CDs dispersion for 24 h. Herein, the concentration of the resulting CDs dispersion was estimated from the weight of the obtained powder. The concentration of the CDs dispersion was adjusted to be 100 mg L−1. The pH of the CDs dispersion was also adjusted to be 7 by using HCl and NaOH solutions before characterization.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were obtained on an UV–visible optical absorption spectrometer (JASCO, V-570). The PL spectra of the CDs dispersions were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (JASCO, FP-6500). The absolute PL QYs of the CDs dispersions at λex = 365 nm were measured with a quantum efficiency measurement system (Otsuka Electronics, QE-2000). The particle sizes and morphologies of the CDs were examined with a field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Tecnai 12). The samples for TEM observation were prepared by drying a drop of CDs dispersions on a copper grid covered with a carbon-reinforced collodion film. X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles were measured on an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Rint-2200) with a Cu Kα radiation source and monochromator. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) absorption spectra of pressed KBr disks containing powder samples were measured using a FT/IR-4200 spectrometer (JASCO). The chemical compositions of the CDs powders were determined using an elemental analyzer (Elementar Analytical, vario EL). Chemical states of carbon of CDs powders were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an XPS instrument (JEOL, JPS-9010TR) with a Mg Kα radiation source. The samples were pressed to form disks for XPS measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of hydrothermal temperature on formation of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs dispersions prepared at different hydrothermal temperatures. The absorption peak 1 at ca. 230 nm is assigned to the π→π∗ transition of CC bonds [8,24,26,27]. The absorption peak 2 at ca. 280 nm is attributed to the n→π∗ transition of CO bonds [26,27]. The absorption peak 3 at ca. 340 nm is attributed to the transition from surface levels to the π∗ level [26,28,29]. These peaks were not observed in the spectrum of the l-cysteine solution ((a) in Fig. 2). The absorbance of these peaks increased as the hydrothermal temperature increased from 110 °C to 230 °C.

Fig. 3 A shows photographs and PL spectra of the CDs dispersions that were used for the UV–vis measurements of Fig. 2A. These dispersions emitted blue light visible to the naked eye. The PL intensity at ca. 434 nm increased with increasing hydrothermal temperature. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the absolute PL QY also increased from 1.1% to 3.0% as the hydrothermal temperature increased from 110 °C to 230 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of hydrothermal duration on formation of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 2B and 3B show the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra, respectively, of the CDs dispersions prepared using different hydrothermal durations. The absorbance in the UV region and the blue emission intensity at ca. 434 nm increased with increasing hydrothermal duration. As shown in Fig. 4B, the PL QY was eventually saturated at 3.1% as the hydrothermal duration increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH of l-cysteine solution on formation of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 2C and 3C show the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra, respectively, of the CDs dispersions synthesized at different pH. For the CDs dispersion prepared at pH 7, the absorbance of the peaks 1–3 and the blue emission intensity were higher than those for the CDs dispersions prepared at pH 1 and pH 13. In contrast, the PL QY of the CDs dispersion prepared at pH 1 was 3.5%, which was higher than the values of 3.0% and 1.5% for the CDs dispersions prepared at pH 7 and pH 13, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4C.

Figs. S5A–S5C display TEM images of the CDs synthesized at different pH. Figs. S5D–S5F show their size distributions measured from the TEM images. The mean particle size and its standard deviation are plotted as a function of pH in Fig. S5G. All of the samples were composed of nanoparticles of ∼6 nm in size irrespective of pH, i.e. the pH did not affect the particle size.

The FT-IR spectra of l-cysteine and the CDs powder samples synthesized at different pH are illustrated in Fig. S6. The characteristic sharp peaks of l-cysteine below 1300 cm−1 and the peak 1 of the stretching vibration of S–H bonds at 2500–2600 cm−1 broadened or disappeared after hydrothermal treatment, indicating thermal decomposition of l-cysteine [25,30–32]. The broad peak 2 at 3000–3200 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds of carboxylic acids [30,33]. The broad peak 3 of the stretching vibration of amide N–H bonds appeared at ca. 3400 cm−1 after hydrothermal treatment [24,30], while this peak was not observed for native l-cysteine, which lacks an amide N–H bond. The peak 4 at ca. 1400 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration deformations of -CH2- [34]. The peak 5 at ca. 1450 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–N bonds [34]. The presence of amide N–H and C–N bonds reveals that the powder samples synthesized at all pH values possessed amide bonds, implying dehydration between the NH2 and COOH groups of l-cysteine molecules during the hydrothermal treatment. The peak 6 at ca. 1560 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of N–H bonds [34]. The broad peak 7 at ca. 1650 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CN, and CC bonds [30,34]. The peak 8 at ca. 1740 cm−1 is assigned to the carbonyl groups in saturated esters [30].

Table S1 shows the proportions of C, H, N, S, and O in the CDs powder samples determined by elemental analysis. Fig. 5 A shows the changes in N/C and S/C molar ratios as a function of synthesis pH. When the synthesis pH decreased from 13 to 1, the N/C molar ratio increased from 0.02 to 0.72, indicating an increase in the amount of N doped into the CDs, while the S/C molar ratio remained constant within experimental error. The carbon contents of the CDs powder samples prepared at pH 1, 7, and 13 were 11.2 at%, 16.2 at%, and 6.9 at%, respectively. This indicates that pH 7 provided the most effective carbonizing conditions in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of l-cysteine concentration on formation of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 2D and 3D show the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra, respectively, for the CDs dispersions prepared at different l-cysteine concentrations. The absorbance of the peaks 1–3 and the blue emission intensity increased with increasing l-cysteine concentration. As shown in Fig. 4D, the PL QY of the CDs dispersions increased from 0.67% to 3.0% as the l-cysteine concentration increased from 33 mmol L−1 to 133 mmol L−1.

Fig. 6 A–E displays TEM images of the CDs prepared at different l-cysteine concentrations. Nanoparticles were observed for all of the samples irrespective of the l-cysteine concentration, while large, irregular plate-like particles were also observed for the sample prepared at 17 mmol L−1 l-cysteine. Fig. 6F–J shows the size distributions of the nanoparticles measured from the TEM images. The mean particle size and its standard deviation are plotted as a function of l-cysteine concentration in Fig. 6K. The mean particle size and its standard deviation decreased with increasing l-cysteine concentration.

Fig. S7 shows XRD profiles of l-cysteine and the CDs powder samples prepared at different l-cysteine concentrations. XRD peaks of crystalline l-cysteine used as the carbon source were not observed for synthesized CDs samples. This result indicates that decomposition of l-cysteine progressed during the hydrothermal treatment. Tiny peaks appeared for the CDs samples; however, they could not be identified.

The FT-IR spectra of the CDs powder samples prepared at different l-cysteine concentrations are illustrated in Fig. 7 . The characteristic sharp peaks of l-cysteine broadened or disappeared with increasing l-cysteine concentration after hydrothermal treatment, indicating thermal decomposition of l-cysteine. The broad peak 3 appeared with increasing l-cysteine concentration. The peak 5, corresponding to the C–N bonds, appeared after hydrothermal treatment. The presence of these peaks reveals that the powder samples possessed amide bonds, implying dehydration between the NH2 and COOH groups of l-cysteine molecules during the hydrothermal treatment. The peak 6 at ca. 1560 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of N–H bonds [34]. The broad peak 7 at ca. 1650 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CN, and CC bonds [30,34].

Fig. S8 shows the C 1s XPS spectra of the CDs powder samples. The C 1s spectra were composed of five peaks: peak 1 at 288.6 eV corresponding to CO and CN bonds, peak 2 at 287.2 eV corresponding to C–S bonds, peak 3 at 286.6 eV corresponding to C–O bonds, peak 4 at 285.5 eV corresponding to C–C bonds, and peak 5 at 284.5 eV corresponding to CC bonds [25,26,35]. Table S2 shows the proportions of different bonds calculated from decomposed C 1s XPS spectra of CDs powders. The proportion of the peak 2 of C–S bonds decreased with the increase in l-cysteine concentration. This result indicates that the decomposition of l-cysteine occurred, resulting in the formation of volatile sulfur compounds. The proportion of the peaks 4 and 5 of C–C and CC bonds increased with increasing l-cysteine concentration, indicating that the carbonization reaction more readily occurred at higher concentration.

Table S1 also shows the proportions of C, H, N, S, and O in the CDs powder samples prepared at different l-cysteine concentrations, determined by elemental analysis. Fig. 5B shows the changes in N/C and S/C molar ratios determined by elemental analysis as a function of l-cysteine concentration. When the concentration increased from 17 mmol L−1 to 133 mmol L−1, the N/C molar ratio increased from 0.32 to 0.54, while the S/C molar ratio decreased from 0.27 to 0.11. It should be noted that the smell of sulfur was released into the air when the reactor was opened after hydrothermal treatment. On increasing the l-cysteine concentration, the smell became stronger. These results indicate that the doping of N into the CDs and the formation of volatile sulfur compounds were promoted with increasing the concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of heating systems on PL intensity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We prepared CDs from l-cysteine under the best optimized synthesis condition (230 °C, 120 min, synthesis pH 7, and 133 mmol L−1) through hydrothermal treatment with an electric heating system as well as a microwave heating system. Fig. S9 shows PL spectra and the photograph of both CDs dispersions. The blue emission intensity of the CDs dispersion prepared using the microwave heating system was 2.7 times higher than that of the CDs dispersion prepared using the electric heating system. This result indicates that homogeneous heating in the microwave heating system facilitated the formation of CDs, leading to the enhanced PL intensity.

The UV–vis absorbance of CC, CO, and CN bonds increased through hydrothermal treatment at increasing temperature and for increasing duration, as shown in Fig. 2A and B, respectively. At the same time, the PL intensity and PL QY also increased, as shown in Fig. 3A and B, and 4A and 4B, respectively. Li et al. suggested that l-cysteine molecules were converted to CDs through the following steps: dehydration condensation between NH2 and COOH groups, pyrolysis, and carbonization (incomplete combustion) in a closed hydrothermal reactor [25]; therefore, the formation of CC, CO, and CN bonds can be attributed to carbonization, oxidation, and doping of nitrogen into CDs, respectively. The formation of these new bonds may influence the PL properties of the l-cysteine-derived CDs through the following three proposed PL mechanisms: (i) emission through direct transitions of the π→π∗ and n→π∗ energy gaps [28,36,37], (ii) emission from energy levels related to the surface states in those energy gaps [28,36,37], and (iii) emission from N-doped levels in those energy gaps [22,35,36]. The CC, CO, and CN bonds may all contribute to the formation of π levels, surface state levels related to those functional groups, and N-doped levels, respectively. The PL of the CDs may therefore be attributed to transitions from the surface state levels and N-doped levels to the π levels.

The dehydration reaction between the NH2 and COOH groups in l-cysteine molecules was suppressed at pH 1 and pH 13 because of the protonation of NH2 groups and the deprotonation of COOH groups, respectively, as shown in Fig. S10. In contrast, dehydration readily occurred at pH 7, resulting in the promotion of a carbonization reaction; therefore, the absorbance and PL intensity at pH 7 were highest among the three pH values, as shown in Figs. 2C and 3C, respectively.

Shu et al. investigated the effect of pH on volatile components in the hydrothermal degradation of cysteine [38]. They found, through experiments conducted at pH 2.2, pH 5.1, and pH 7.1, that the formation of volatile compounds from sulfur-containing, nitrogen-lacking hydrocarbons was more favorable at lower pH. This implies that the loss of carbon and sulfur should be greater at lower pH, resulting in the increase in the N/C molar ratio, as verified in Fig. 5A. Dong et al. suggested that N-doped levels can facilitate radiative recombination [22]; therefore, the increased emission from CDs prepared under acidic conditions may be attributed to increased doping of N, which contributed to the CDs prepared at pH1 having the highest PL QY, as shown in Fig. 4C.

The UV–vis absorbance of CC, CO, and CN bonds increased through hydrothermal treatment at increasing l-cysteine concentration, as shown in Fig. 2D. At the same time, the PL intensity and PL QY also increased, as shown in Figs. 3D and 4D, respectively. At higher l-cysteine concentration, the dehydration reaction between l-cysteine molecules more readily occurred, resulting in the promotion of the subsequent carbonization reaction. This improved the PL intensity and PL QY.

At the lowest concentration of 17 mmol L−1, large, irregular plate-like particles were observed, as shown in Fig. 6A. They were probably peptides formed by polymerization through repeated dehydration condensation of l-cysteine [39]. The decomposition of these peptides produced smaller-sized CDs, as shown in Fig. 6B–E. As the decomposition progressed, volatilization of sulfur-containing hydrocarbons lacking nitrogen increased the N/C molar ratio, as shown in Fig. 5B. The relationship between the formation process of CDs and their properties is summarized in Fig. 8 .

CDs were prepared from x mmol L−1 (17 ≤ x ≤ 133) aqueous solutions of l-cysteine whose pH values were adjusted to be y (y = 1, 7, or 13) through hydrothermal treatment at T °C (110 ≤ T ≤ 230) for t min (5 ≤ t ≤ 120) using a microwave heating system. The absorbance of transitions related to CC, CO, and CN bonds, and the PL intensity of blue emission increased through hydrothermal treatment at increasing temperature and for increasing duration. The PL QY of the CDs increased from 1.1% to 3.0%, and gradually became saturated at 3.1% with an increase in the hydrothermal temperature from 110 °C to 230 °C, and the duration from 5 min to 120 min. The formation of a greater number of CC, CO, and CN bonds through carbonization, oxidation, and doping of nitrogen into the CDs, respectively, apparently enhanced the PL intensity of the transitions from surface state levels and N-doped levels to π levels. The absorbance and the PL intensity of the CDs prepared at pH 7 were the highest among the three pH values of 1, 7, and 13. The protonation of NH2 groups at pH 1 and the deprotonation of COOH groups at pH 13 suppressed the dehydration reaction between l-cysteine molecules. Conversely, at pH 7, more efficient dehydration prompted more efficient carbonization as compared with other pH values, thus maximizing the absorbance and PL intensity. The absorbance and the PL intensity of the CDs also increased when increasing the l-cysteine concentration used for their synthesis. The PL QY increased from 0.67% to 3.0% as the concentration increased from 33 mmol L−1 to 133 mmol L−1. Higher l-cysteine concentrations may have facilitated the dehydration reaction between the molecules and the subsequent carbonization, contributing to the improved optical properties of the CDs. These results verified that the promotion of the dehydration reaction of the starting material is a key factor to enhance the optical properties of CDs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.05.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Jiang, Y. He, S. Li, H. Cui, Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement, Chem. Commun. 48 (2012) 9634-9636. https://doi.org/10.1039/C2CC34612E.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Wang, Z. Xu, H. Cheng, H. Lin, M. G. Humphrey, C. Zhang, A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature, Carbon 82 (2015) 87-95. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.10.035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glucose-derived carbonaceous nanospheres for photoacoustic imaging and photothermal therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.-H. Miao, H. Wang, H. Yang, Z. Li, L. Zhen, C.-Y. Xu, Glucose-derived carbonaceous nanospheres for photoacoustic imaging and photothermal therapy, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2016) 15904-15910. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.6b03652.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green preparation of carbon dots by Jinhua bergamot for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yu, N. Song, Y.-K. Zhang, S.-X. Zhong, A.-J. Wang, J. Chen, Green preparation of carbon dots by Jinhua bergamot for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+, Sens. Actuators, B 214 (2015) 29-35. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.03.006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-P. Sun, B. Zhou, Y. Lin, W. Wang, K. A. S. Fernando, P. Pathak, M. J. Meziani, B. A. Harruff, X. Wang, H. Wang, P. G. Luo, H. Yang, M. E. Kose, B. Chen, L. M. Veca, S.-Y. Xie, Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 128 (2006) 7756-7757. https://doi.org/10.1021/ja062677d.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, T. Ye, C. Mao, Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 46 (2007) 6473-6475. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.200701271.
